results.possibleIdentity: Arthur "Bill" Brody (born March 2, 1943) is an American artist, printmaker, and educator known for his pioneering work in both traditional plein air landscape painting and computer-generated art. He earned a B.S. in Mathematics from Harvey Mudd College (1965) and an M.F.A. in Painting and Printmaking from Claremont Graduate School (1967). Brody moved to Fairbanks, Alaska, in 1967 to teach at the University of Alaska Fairbanks (UAF), where he remained until 1969. During the early 1970s (1970–1975), he taught at Ripon College in Wisconsin before returning to UAF in 1977. At UAF, he developed the computer art and printmaking curricula and served as the department chair. His work is characterized by a unique intersection of art and technology; he served as a research specialist at UAF's Arctic Region Supercomputing Center, where he explored virtual reality and led the Body Language User Interface (BLUI) project. His association with the experimental media and technology movement of the 1960s and 1970s is evidenced by his inclusion in the Phil Morton Memorial Research Archive, which documents the emergence of experimental video and media art. Brody's artistic output includes large-scale oil paintings created in situ in remote Alaskan wilderness and complex carved and painted copper panels. His work is held in major collections, including the Brooklyn Museum, the Portland Art Museum, and the Anchorage Museum.

results.possibleIdentity: Susan Alexander was a dancer and member of the Merce Cunningham Dance Company during the late 1960s and early 1970s. She participated in several landmark performances that involved collaborations between artists, engineers, and scientists, which were characteristic of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement. Notable works she performed in include 'Scramble' (1967) and 'RainForest' (1968), the latter of which featured a set of floating Mylar pillows designed by Andy Warhol. Her name appears in various archives and documents associated with E.A.T. and the Merce Cunningham Foundation, reflecting her activity in the avant-garde performance scene of that era.

Susan Alexander (also known as Susan Alexjander) is a composer, sound artist, and biomusician known for her work at the intersection of art and science. She is famous for creating 'science-based' soundscapes by mapping scientific data—such as DNA frequencies (in her 1988 project 'Sequencia') and atomic 'Larmor frequencies'—into audible musical tones. Her work is explicitly linked to the legacy of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) in reports by institutions like the Smithsonian, which cite her as an example of interdisciplinary collaboration between art and science. She has collaborated with various artists, including Rebecca Kamen on the installation 'Divining Nature: An Elemental Garden' (2009).

Susan Alexander (born 1929) is a prominent New York-born artist and sculptor who became a resident of Jamaica in 1951. She is well-known for her bronze sculptures and paintings depicting Jamaican life and culture. Her work, such as 'Flower Sisters' and 'Glory Glory be,' is held in the collection of the National Gallery of Jamaica. While she was highly active during the 1960s and 1970s, her artistic focus has traditionally been on figurative sculpture and painting rather than the technological collaborations typically associated with E.A.T.

results.possibleIdentity: Xavier de la Salle is a French artist and architect active during the late 1960s and 1970s. He is best known as a co-founder of Group Ludic (Groupe Ludic), established in 1968 (or 1967) alongside David Roditi and Simon Koszel. The collective specialized in creating experimental, modular, and sculptural play environments for children, which they treated as architectural and artistic experiments. De la Salle's work is deeply rooted in the collaboration between art, psychology (notably the theories of Jean Piaget), and technology. In July 1968, he published a significant article titled 'Structures de Jeux' (Play Structures) in Leonardo, the premier international journal for artists working with science and technology, reflecting an intellectual association with the same movements that fostered the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). His work often involved modular polyurethane and polyester sculptures and participatory workshops that were pioneering in the field of urban experimental art. From 1973 to 2001, he served as a Professor of Design, Environment, and Urbanism at the École d'Architecture de Paris-La Défense.

Xavier Esqueda is a prominent Mexican artist born in 1943, recognized for his work in the late 1960s and 1970s involving Op-Art and Surrealist elements. He was a member of the 'Ruptura' and 'Los Grupos' movements in Mexico, which sought to intervene in traditional art structures through conceptual and experimental practices. Esqueda has a direct association with the landmark Art and Technology movements of the era; he was featured in the 1971 report of the 'Art and Technology' program at the Los Angeles County Museum of Art (LACMA). This program, which ran from 1967 to 1971, was a major parallel and collaborative peer to the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization in New York, often involving the same artists (such as Robert Rauschenberg) and focused on similar goals of pairing artists with corporate engineering and scientific resources. Esqueda's 1968 series 'Present Memories' is frequently cited as a significant historical contribution to this era of experimental Latin American art.

results.possibleIdentity: Michael William "Mike" Hart (1943–2016) was an English singer-songwriter, poet, and prominent figure in the Liverpool countercultural art scene of the late 1960s and early 1970s. He first gained recognition as the leader of the rhythm and blues band The Roadrunners, performing at venues like the Cavern Club. In the late 1960s, he became a founding member of The Liverpool Scene, an influential multidisciplinary collective that blended poetry, music, and performance art, alongside figures like Adrian Henri and Andy Roberts. After leaving the group, he launched a solo career on John Peel's Dandelion Records, releasing the acclaimed and eccentric album 'Mike Hart Bleeds' (1969) and 'Basher, Chalky, Pongo and Me' (1972). His work was noted for its raw, folk-rock style and poetic social commentary.

Michael Stern Hart (1947–2011) was a technologist and futurist widely credited with inventing the e-book and founding Project Gutenberg in 1971. While primarily known for his technological contributions, he is frequently cited in historical accounts of the intersection between art and technology (such as the 'Johns Hopkins Guide to Digital Media') alongside organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). His pioneering work at the University of Illinois Materials Research Lab involved digitizing classic texts (starting with the Declaration of Independence) as a form of "Replicator Technology," a concept that mirrored the goal of making art and information accessible through technical collaboration. He was also briefly a street musician and expressed his philosophical views on talent and control through digital platforms.

Mike Hart (nicknamed "Molten Mick") was a biologist and artist active in the early 1970s as a member of 'Liquid Len and the Lensmen,' a pioneering light show collective. The group was renowned for providing ground-breaking, technically advanced visual displays for the space-rock band Hawkwind. Their shows involved a complex mix of film loops, projections, and animations, representing a significant collaboration between artistic vision and technical engineering during the psychedelic era. Hart eventually left the touring circuit to pursue a career as a veterinary hematologist.
